title: Edge-statistics Conjecture (Conjecture 1.1)
statement_text: For all integers $k$ and $\ell$ with $0 < \ell < \binom{k}{2}$, we have $\mathrm{ind}(k,\ell) \leq \frac{1}{e} + o_k(1)$.
context_text: Alon, Hefetz, Krivelevich and Tyomkyn introduced the edge-inducibility $\mathrm{ind}(k,\ell) = \lim_{n\to\infty} I(n,k,\ell)$ and conjectured this universal upper bound. The constant $1/e$ is tight: considering $G(n,p)$ with $p = 1/\binom{k}{2}$ gives $\mathrm{ind}(k,1) \geq e^{-1}+o_k(1)$, and a complete bipartite graph gives $\mathrm{ind}(k,k-1) \geq e^{-1}+o_k(1)$.
paper_contribution: Completes the proof of the conjecture by handling the remaining cases $1 \leq \ell \leq C \cdot k$ (Theorem 1.2), together with the earlier result of Kwan, Sudakov and Tran which covered $C \cdot k \leq \ell \leq \binom{k}{2}/2$.
paper_title: A Completion of the Proof of the Edge-statistics Conjecture

title: Hypergraph Edge-statistics Conjecture
statement_text: For fixed $r$, $\mathrm{ind}_r(k,\ell) \leq e^{-1} + o_k(1)$ for all $0 < \ell < \binom{k}{r}$.
context_text: Alon et al. suggested this natural $r$-uniform hypergraph analogue of the Edge-statistics Conjecture. The lower bound $\mathrm{ind}_r(k,\ell) \geq e^{-1}+o_k(1)$ holds whenever $\ell = \binom{k-s}{r-s}$ for some $1 \leq s \leq r$, confirming that the constant $e^{-1}$ would be tight. The paper also extends results to not-necessarily-uniform hypergraphs with edge size bounded by $r$.
paper_contribution: Proves the conjecture for the sublinear range $1 \leq \ell < k/r$ via Theorem 1.4, establishing $\mathrm{ind}_r(k,\ell) \leq \mathrm{ind}_{\leq r}(k,\ell) \leq \frac{k}{k-r\ell} \cdot \frac{1}{e}$, which yields $e^{-1}+o_k(1)$ when $r\ell = o(k)$; further quantitative bounds for the range $1 \leq \ell \leq (1-\varepsilon)k/r$ are given in Theorem 1.5.
notes: Described in the paper as 'a natural analogue of Conjecture 1.1, suggested by Alon et al.' rather than given a formal label; statement reconstructed from prose.
paper_title: A Completion of the Proof of the Edge-statistics Conjecture
